coredata.dc:title: Novel nanobelts constructed from hexagonal graphene quantum dots: Electronic, optical, and sensing properties
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Abdelsalam, Hazem
Abd-Elkader, Omar H.
Sakr, Mahmoud A.S.
Teleb, Nahed H.
Osman, W.
Zhilong, Wang
Zhang, Qinfang
coredata.dc:description: New nanobelts and nanoribbons are built from zigzag-hexagonal graphene quantum dots. Their stability, electronic, optical, and sensing properties are studied using density functional theory calculations. The infrared spectra's real vibrational frequencies and positive binding energy prove the stability of the constructed structures. The thermal stability at 600 K is also confirmed by molecular dynamics simulations. The nanobelts have a significantly low energy gap compared to the planer single quantum dots or the extended nanoribbons based on these nanodots. This is a result of the wrapping of the graphene nanodots which raises the π-overlapping between adjacent rings that decreases the energy gap. Interactive electronic states localized at the zigzag edges characterize both nanobelts and nanoribbons. Thus these systems are promising candidates for sensing and catalytic applications. Their sensing capability is tested by studying the adsorption of selected volatile organic compounds such as chloromethane and trichloroethylene. The findings indicate that these gases are effectively adsorbed with moderate adsorption energy and charge transfer, opening the door toward gas sensor applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nanobelts and nanoribbons
DFT
Electronic and optical properties
Volatile organic compounds
Sensors

This work is supported also by Researchers Supporting Project number ( RSP2024R468 ), King Saud University , Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)The optimized structures of the hexagonal graphene quantum dot (ZHG) used to build various nanobelts (NB). Namely from two ZHG (b), three ZHG (c), four ZHG (d), five ZHG (e), and (f) six ZHG (f). (g, h) Selected two nanoribbons from three and four ZHG.
Electronic density of states of selected nanobelts and nanoribbons based on ZHG.
The HOMO and LUMO distributions for selected nanobelts (a-c) and nanoribbons (d-e).
Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) of ZHG-based materials.
Calculated absorbance spectra for different NBs and NRs in the gaseous state.
The electronic density of states 4ZHG-NB after adsorption of chloromethane (a, b), Trichloroethylene (c,d), and Vinyl chloride (e, f) at the edges and the links.
Some important quantum properties for ZHG and its based NBs and NRs such as bond length (Å), dihedral, and bond angles (degrees).
The calculated excited state (ES), maximum wavelength (λmax), transition energy (TE), electronic transition (ET), oscillator strength (f), and transition coefficient (TC) for some Nbs and NRs.
Adsorption energy (Ea), adsorption distance, and energy gap (Eg) of the complexes after adsorption of VOCs.
Natural charge, population, and electronic configuration of chloride atoms for volatile organic compounds and 4ZHG-NB after adsorption of volatile organic compounds.

head.ce:title: Novel nanobelts constructed from hexagonal graphene quantum dots: Electronic, optical, and sensing properties

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: New nanobelts and nanoribbons are built from zigzag-hexagonal graphene quantum dots. Their stability, electronic, optical, and sensing properties are studied using density functional theory calculations. The infrared spectra's real vibrational frequencies and positive binding energy prove the stability of the constructed structures. The thermal stability at 600 K is also confirmed by molecular dynamics simulations. The nanobelts have a significantly low energy gap compared to the planer single quantum dots or the extended nanoribbons based on these nanodots. This is a result of the wrapping of the graphene nanodots which raises the π-overlapping between adjacent rings that decreases the energy gap. Interactive electronic states localized at the zigzag edges characterize both nanobelts and nanoribbons. Thus these systems are promising candidates for sensing and catalytic applications. Their sensing capability is tested by studying the adsorption of selected volatile organic compounds such as chloromethane and trichloroethylene. The findings indicate that these gases are effectively adsorbed with moderate adsorption energy and charge transfer, opening the door toward gas sensor applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanocarbons refer to carbon-based materials that have nanoscale dimensions [1–3]. They come in a variety of forms, including fullerenes, nanorings and nanobelts, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and graphene. Due to their exceptional properties, carbon nanomaterials, particularly CNTs and graphene, have received a lot of attention lately as cutting-edge materials for innovative technological applications [4–7]. For instance, CNTs have excellent mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties, making them useful in a wide range of applications such as electronics, energy storage, composite materials, and biological applications [8–10]. Graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice, is known for its exceptional strength, electrical conductivity, and thermal conductivity [11–13]. Graphene has potential applications in electronics, energy storage, sensors, and many other fields [14,15]. Other interesting examples of nanocarbons are nanobelts and nanorings due to distinct features resulting from the highly strained cyclic structure, high symmetry, and cyclic -systems[16–18]. Because of this, they demonstrate intriguing uses in a variety of applications, including electronics, sensors, biological applications, and environmental applications [19–23].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanoflakes of graphene that have dimensions on the order of a few nanometers. they possess distinctive electric and optical features due mainly to the quantum confinement effects, edge type, and chemical functionalization [24–28]. GQDs can be fabricated using several techniques, such as chemical exfoliation [29], laser ablation [30], electrochemical [31], and on-surface [32] techniques. These methods enable the fabrication of graphene quantum dots with different shapes and edge terminations such as hexagonal [33] and triangular [34,35] graphene quantum dots. Which provide them with many interesting electronic, magnetic, and optical properties [31,36–41] rendering them promising for different applications including electronics [42,43], spintronics [44,45], sensors [46,47], energy [48,49], water treatment [50,51].

In this work, we construct novel nanobelts by connecting zigzag-hexagonal graphene quantum dots (ZHG) termination and wrapping them to form a cylindrical shape with different diameters depending on the size of the ZHG. Nanoribbons from ZHG are also considered and compared with the nanobelts. The density functional theory calculations show that the electronic structure of these nanobelts is significantly different from the constituent hexagonal graphene nanodots. For instance, the single ZHG considered here has an energy gap equal to ∼2.9 eV [52]. This gap significantly decreases to ∼0.8 eV by wrapping four ZHG into nanobelts. On the other hand, the decrease of the energy gap in the nanoribbons is not that high with an energy gap of ∼1.2 eV for the same number of connected ZHG. The sensing ability of these nanobelts toward volatile organic compounds (VOCs) such as chloromethane and trichloroethylene is also tested. The results show that the nanobelts exhibited effective adsorption of volatile organic compounds (VOCs), supported by adsorption energies and distances. Importantly, this adsorption process had minimal impact on electronic and optical properties, reinforcing the physical nature of the interaction. Natural Bond Orbital (NBO) analysis revealed notable shifts in charge distribution during VOC adsorption, providing insights into charge transfer mechanisms. These findings, consistent with the literature on graphene-based materials, underscore the potential of ZHG nanobelts in gas sensing applications and contribute to a comprehensive understanding of their versatile properties[53–56].

The structural optimization and calculations of electronic and optical properties of the considered finite systems are examined using density functional theory as implemented in Gaussian 16 [57]. The Becke-3-parameter-Lee-Yang-Parr (B3LYP) functional is used for our DFT calculations [58,59] with the addition of Grimme’s dispersion correction (gd3) to take into account the Van der Waals interactions [60]. The B3LYP functional is taken into consideration because it accurately depicts the electrical structure of C-based nanomaterials [25,61–63]. The basis set used here is the 6–31 g [64,65] which shows acceptable results for C-based nanomaterials when considering both the required processing power and the accuracy [49,66]. To determine the optical properties, the first 20 excited states are investigated using time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) simulations.

The zigzag hexagonal graphene quantum dots (simply, ZHG) that will be used to build the nanobelts and nanoribbons are shown in Fig. 1 (a). The nanobelt (NB) constructed from 2ZHG is referred to as 2ZHG-NB as shown in Fig. 1 (b). We then increase the number of ZHG in the nanobelts to include 3ZHG, 4ZHG…, and 6ZHG (Fig. 1 c-f) to study the effect of changing the diameter on the electronic and optical properties. Planer nanoribbons (NR) from 3ZHG…, and 6ZHG are also considered and compared with the nanobelts, see Fig. 1 (g, h).

Fig. 1 and Table 1 depict the optimized structures of ZHG in both nanobelt (NB) and nanoribbon (NR) morphologies, offering detailed information about bond lengths, bond angles, and dihedral angles. In all examined ZHG-NR structures a flat surface is evident where all carbon atoms lie in the same plane. This is supported by dihedral angles such as C2-C3-C4-C9 and C10-C6-C7-C8, which are consistently at 180.00 degrees. Conversely, all examined ZHG-NB structures exhibit inner and outer flat surfaces but with carbon atoms not lying in the same plane due to the formation of rings in the NB structure. This is evidenced by dihedral angles like C2-C3-C4-C9 and C10-C6-C7-C8, which fall within the range of 151.022 to 170.872 degrees. Changes in the number of ZHG units in both NB and NR configurations result in variations in intermolecular bond lengths, specifically C3-C6 and C6-C10 bonds. However, these changes do not significantly affect the C1-C2 bond length as shown in Table 1. When transitioning from NBs to NRs, the C3-C6 and C6-C10 bond lengths increase by 0.002 and 0.001 angstroms, respectively. Table 1 also provides insight into the bond angles C1-C2-C3 and C7-C6-C10, which indicate sp2 hybridization.

A comprehensive investigation to assess the stability of ZHG nanobelts (NBs) and nanoribbons (NRs) by calculating their binding energy (BE) and vibrational frequencies has been conducted. The binding energy was determined using the equation: BE = (NCEC + NHEH - Et)/Nt, where NC, NH, and Nt represent the numbers of carbon, hydrogen, and the total number of atoms, respectively. EC, EH, and Et correspond to the total energies of the carbon atoms, hydrogen atoms, and the final compound, respectively. Our analysis of the calculated binding energies revealed positive values ranging from 12.12 to 12.39 eV, suggesting the formation of the studied NBs and NRs in a stable manner. We further evaluated the binding energies for various ZHG-based structures, including 2ZHG-NB, 3ZHG-NB, 4ZHG-NB, 5ZHG-NB, 6ZHG-NB, 3ZHG-NR, 4ZHG-NR, 5ZHG-NR, and 6ZHG-NR. The calculated values of BE for these structures were 12.31, 12.36, 12.38, 12.39, 12.39, 12.12, 12.19, 12.23, and 12.26 eV, respectively. Notably, these results demonstrate that as the number of ZHG units in both NBs and NRs increases, the stability of these molecular structures (MSs) also increases. Moreover, we observed that the stability of NBs, as indicated by the values of BE, is higher than that of NRs. In conclusion, based on the calculated values of BE, our findings suggest that all the considered structures are energetically stable with comparable binding energies.

Calculating a structure's vibrational frequency and determining whether it is real or imaginary is another crucial step in confirming the dynamical stability of the systems under consideration. Fig. S1 shows the infrared spectra of the nanoribbons and nanobelts that were constructed from hexagonal GQDs. The plots demonstrate real (positive) vibrational frequencies which suggest that the optimized structures represent a local minimum on the potential energy surface of the B3LYB functional that ensures the dynamical stability. The thermal stability of selected ZHG nanobelts and nanoribbons is also investigated using ab initio molecular dynamics simulations. The centered density matrix propagation (ADMP) molecular dynamics model is employed for the simulation[67,68]. The results are displayed in Fig. S2 which presents the potential energy and bond length fluctuations as a function of time at 600 K. The trajectories demonstrate that during ten femtoseconds at this high temperature, the potential energy and the bond length slightly change. Thus, the ZHG-based nanobetls and nanoribbons are thermally stable at 600 K.

The electronic properties are investigated by considering the density of states (DOS), energy gap, and the distributions of the highest occupied/lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (HOMO/LUMO). The DOS of the nanoribbons and nanobelts based on ZHG under consideration is depicted in Fig. 2 . The DOS is computed using a Gaussian distribution,

( ∈ -
∈ i
)

2
]
with a broadening of
α = 0.32 e V
for each molecular orbital (
∈ i
). Interestingly, the wide energy gap of the single ZHG decreases from 2.9 eV to 0.77 eV after wrapping only two ZHG to form 2ZHG-NB as shown in Fig. 2 (a). This significant decrease is mainly a result of wrapping the ZHG. Where the strain energy becomes larger by increasing the curvature of the structure which in turn forces the torsional angle to decrease to account for the increased strain. The π-overlap between adjacent rings rises as these angles decrease and this could account for the noticeable decrease in the HOMO-LUMO energy gap [69]. Consequently, increasing the diameter (decreasing the curvature) of the nanobelts or converting it to nanoribbons would lead to an increase in the energy gap that is proportional to the degree of planarity of the structure.

This explanation is confirmed by the higher energy gaps obtained for 3ZHG…6ZHG (Fig. 2 b-e) and for the planer nanoribbons shown in Fig. 2 (f-i). In the planer cases, the observed decrease of the energy gap from 1.34 eV to 1.02 eV by increasing the size is simply due to the quantum confinement effect. From the HOMO and LUMO distributions presented in Fig. 3 for selected NBs and NRs, we can gain some insights into the low-energy molecular orbitals.

First for small NBs such as 2ZHG-NB the HOMO and LUMO represent delocalized π-bond between neighbour C-atoms. Increasing the diameter of the NBs, starting from 4ZHG-NB, the HOMO and LUMO distributions become localized on single C-atoms, especially on the edges as seen in Fig. 6 (c). These electronic states are called edge states that are localized on the zigzag edges and significantly improve the edge activity. The same also occurs in the planer NRs (Fig. 3 d, e) but the number of edge states is higher in NBs which could be a result of the higher π-overlap between adjacent rings in NBs. Thus NBs are expected to be more efficient as catalysts or sensors due to the higher active states at the edges.

Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) studies play a pivotal role in unveiling the electrophilic and nucleophilic characteristics of ZHG structures in both nanobelt (NB) and nanoribbon (NR) morphologies. These studies offer invaluable insights into predicting reaction sites [70]. In Fig. 4 , we present MEPs for ZHG in both NB and NR configurations. In these representations, negatively charged regions are depicted in red, positively charged areas in blue, and neutral zones in green. Within ZHG-NRs, the negative regions (in red) are predominantly concentrated at the edge. Also, in ZHG-NBs, these negative regions are centered on the inner and outer edges making the edges active as we discussed before. This observation suggests that these regions act as nucleophilic sites, indicating their tendency to donate electrons during chemical reactions. On the other hand, the positive regions (in blue) in the MEPs are mainly situated around the terminal hydrogen atoms.

These regions signify electrophilic sites, indicating their readiness to accept electrons in chemical reactions. Finally, the neutral regions (in green) in the MEPs correspond to areas with no significant charge distribution, signifying their relative electron neutrality. Collectively, the MEP analysis provides valuable insights into the electrophilic and nucleophilic tendencies of distinct regions within ZHG in both NB and NR morphologies. This information enhances our understanding of their reactivity and potential reactivity sites, aiding in predicting and comprehending their chemical behavior and interactions across various applications.

Fig. 5 visually represents the calculated absorbance spectra for various ZHG nanobelts (NBs) and nanoribbons (NRs) in the gaseous state. Table 2 presents crucial optical parameters characterizing these materials. Examining Table 2, it becomes apparent that NBs exhibit a blueshift in the maximum absorption wavelength (λmax) compared to NRs. For instance, 3ZHG-NB has a λmax of 727.80 nm, while 3ZHG-NR has a longer-wavelength absorption at 984.99 nm. This shift can be attributed to several factors gleaned from the data in Table 2. In NBs, the larger size and extended conjugation result in lower transition energies (TE) but higher oscillator strengths (f). Conversely, NRs exhibit longer-wavelength absorbance due to their confined geometry, which leads to lower transition energies as indicated in Table 2. The simple “H → L” electronic transitions for NRs, as shown in Table 2, also contribute to this trend. Therefore, by considering the interplay between ZHG size, structure, electronic transitions, and the data in Table 2, we gain valuable insights into the optical behavior of these materials (Table 2; Fig. 5).

The sensing properties of the constructed nanobelts are investigated here by considering the adsorption of volatile organic compounds (VOCs), namely chloromethane (CM), Trichloroethylene (TCE), and Vinyl chloride (VC). We consider the adsorption on the 4ZHG-NB as an example of the NBs to test the adsorption process where at this size the effect of edges starts to appear. The adsorption of VOCs is considered on the edges, the links connecting the ZHG, and the surface to compare the adsorption strength. The adsorption energy which is defined as the difference between the product energy and the reactant energies and the adsorption length (d) is calculated and tabulated in Table 3 . First, we see that the values of the adsorption energies are negative indicating the energetically stable adsorption of the VOCs. Second, the adsorption energy has moderate values ranging from 0.25 eV to 0.71 eV the low values are for the CM adsorption while the middle and high ones are for the adsorption of VC and TCE, respectively. The higher adsorption strength in cases of VC and TCE is a result of the higher interactions between them and the NBs due to the higher number of Cl atoms that boost the charge transfer as will be indicated in the next section. The high adsorption distances given in Table 3 indicate that VOCs are physically adsorbed without the formation of any chemical bonds.

The calculated energy gaps in Table 3 and the partial density of states in Fig. 6 are used to investigate the effect of adsorping VOCs on the electronic properties. The HOMO and LUMO distributions are also considered and added as insets to Fig. 6. It is observed that the adsorped molecules have a tiny effect on the PDOS with almost no change in density peaks in the low energy region. The energy gap of 0.81 eV is almost unaffected also by the adsorption. The density peaks from the adsorped VOCs appear deeper in the density spectrum, see the red peaks in Fig. 6.

It is worth noting that we consider only three organic pollutants to represent different VOCs. In general, VOCs are classified according to their volatility into three groups: (a) Very volatile organic compounds (b) Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) (c) Semi volatile organic compounds[71]. The first group is the most harmful pollutants because even at extremely low concentrations, they are poisonous. This group contains propane, butane, chloromethane, etc. The second group includes vinyl chloride, formaldehyde, toluene, isopropyl alcohol, etc. The third class which has higher molecular weight and boiling points consists of pesticides, chlordane, benzyl alcohol, etc. The investigated organic pollutants in this work represent examples from these main groups. For instance, Chloromethane represents organic pollutants from the first group. However, to provide further justification for the ability of the constructed nanotube to adsorb different VOCs, we show in Fig. S3 the optimized structures of 4ZHG-NB adsorbing additional organic pollutants benzene, toluene, and benzyl alcohol. The adsorption energies indicate successful adsorption of these pollutants with moderate adsorption energy of −0.44, −0.55, and −0.75 eV for benzene, toluene, and benzyl alcohol, respectively.

The impact of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) adsorption on the optical properties of 4ZHG-NB is investigated. We observe slight changes in the absorption spectra and optical parameters. Prior to adsorption, the maximum absorption wavelength (λmax) of 4ZHG-NB was approximately 848.23 nm, while the transition energy (TE) was around 1.462 eV. The corresponding main electronic transition (ET) was described as “H → L + 3.” The oscillator strength (f) and transition coefficient (TC) were 1.410 and 0.564, respectively. After VOC adsorption, tiny shifts in λmax are observed, See Fig. S4 and Table S1, such as 848.30 nm with CM and 848.54 nm with VC, indicating the weak physical interactions between 4ZHG-NB and the VOCs. TE remains relatively constant for most VOCs but shows minor variations, like a TE of 1.467 eV after TCE adsorption, indicating changes in electronic structure as given in Table S1. Importantly, ET remains consistent post-adsorption, while f values exhibit minor fluctuations (e.g., an increase to 1.463 with VC), implying alterations in the probability of electronic transitions. Likewise, TC values show slight variations, with a decrease to 0.537 after TCE adsorption, suggesting an influence on the intensity of electronic transitions. In summary, the optical properties similar to the electronic ones indicate the moderate physical adsorption of VOCs by the considered nanobelts.

The Natural Bond Orbital (NBO) analysis employed in this study offers valuable insights into the charge distribution and electronic configuration of chloride atoms, both before and after the adsorption as given in Table 4 . The observed alterations in natural charge, population, and electronic configuration of chloride atoms provide a comprehensive understanding of the charge transfer and interactions between 4ZHG-NB and the adsorbed VOCs. Specifically, when examining CM adsorption, before interaction, the chloride atom exhibits a natural charge of −0.06533 and an electronic configuration of [core]3S(1.86)3p(5.19)4p(0.01), see Table 4. Following adsorption on 4ZHG-NB-CM-edg, the chloride atom experiences a substantial increase in negative charge, registering at −0.12899. This pronounced shift indicates a notable transfer of electron density from the chloride atom to the material, strongly implying robust interactions and charge transfer. In contrast, during VC adsorption, the chloride atom's initial natural charge is −0.06068, and its electronic configuration is [core]3S(1.88)3p(5.17). After adsorption on 4ZHG-NB-VC-edg, the chloride atom displays a slightly more negative charge of −0.06907. While this increment in charge still suggests electron density transfer, it hints at a comparatively weaker interaction compared to CM. Lastly, examining TCE adsorption, the chloride atom exhibits a positive natural charge of 0.02764 before adsorption, implying that it has accumulated some electron density. Post adsorption on 4ZHG-NB-TCE-edg, the chloride atom maintains a positive charge, albeit at a reduced value of 0.01174. This phenomenon hints at a potential charge transfer from the chloride atom to the material. In summary, the NBO analysis effectively demonstrates that the adsorption of volatile organic compounds onto 4ZHG-NB results in notable shifts in the natural charge of chloride atoms. The magnitude and direction of these shifts differ depending on the type of VOCs. CM adsorption elicits a significant increase in the negative charge on the chloride atom, indicating a substantial charge transfer and a robust interaction. In the case of VC, the charge transfer is less pronounced, indicative of a weaker interaction. TCE adsorption, on the other hand, maintains the chloride atom's positive charge, suggesting a conceivable interaction and charge transfer from the chloride atom to the material. These findings serve as compelling evidence of the adsorption of volatile organic compounds on 4ZHG-NB and provide critical insights into the nature of charge transfer within these interactions, holding significant implications for various applications, including environmental remediation and gas sensing.

Novel nanobelts and nanoribbons based on zigzag-hexagonal graphene quantum dots (ZHG) have been constructed and investigated using density functional theory calculations. The stability of the considered structures is verified by the positive binding energy and the real vibrational frequencies obtained from the infrared spectra. Moreover, the thermal stability at 600 K is confirmed by molecular dynamics simulations. The electronic calculations show that the nanobelts have a tiny energy gap of 0.8 eV which is significantly lower than the gap in the planer ZHG or the extended nanoribbons based on it. The noticeable decrease in the energy gap is a result of the increased π-overlap between adjacent rings due to the wrapping of ZHG to form the nanobelts. Both the nanobelts and the nanoribbons are characterized by interactive electronic states localized at the zigzag edges however they are slightly higher in nanobelts. Thus the constructed systems are potential candidates for sensing and catalytic applications due to these active edge states. The adsorption of selected volatile organic compounds was tested and the results show that the nanobelts successfully adsorb these gases with moderate adsorption energy and charge transfer.

Hazem Abdelsalam: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Omar H. Abd-Elkader: Software, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Mahmoud A.S. Sakr: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Nahed H. Teleb: Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. W. Osman: Validation, Software, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wang Zhilong: Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Qinfang Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12274361), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20211361), and College Natural Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province (20KJA430004). This work is supported also by Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R468), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rinp.2024.107412.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular nanocarbon science: Present and future

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Itami, K. and T. Maekawa, Molecular nanocarbon science: Present and future. 2020, ACS Publications. p. 4718-4720.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical synthesis of carbon nanorings and nanobelts
